summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Mark Russinovich's concerns about the memory footprint of .NET managed applications compared to native code. While acknowledging that managed apps do consume more resources, Atwood argues this is a reasonable tradeoff because developer productivity matters more than raw performance. He points out that the set of tasks requiring low-level languages shrinks as hardware improves, and that a developer can create multiple managed applications in the time it takes to write one native app. Atwood conducts his own measurements of .NET's baseline footprint and concludes the overhead is acceptable given the productivity gains.
key_points: The debate over managed code overhead has existed for decades and represents a fundamental tradeoff between performance and developer productivity
Hardware continues to improve while human cognitive capacity remains constant, making developer time increasingly valuable relative to machine resources
A talented developer can write several managed .NET apps in the time required for one unmanaged C++ application
The baseline memory footprint of minimal .NET applications (4-12 MB) is reasonable given the productivity benefits
Russinovich's comparison of managed vs native Notepad, while technically accurate, misses the larger economic picture of software development
money_quotes: Computers get faster every day, but our brains don't.
The goal of the .NET runtime is not to squeeze every drop of performance out of the platform – it's to make software development easier.
Would you rather have a single fast native app, or a dozen slower managed apps to choose from?
The subset of tasks that must be done in (insert favorite low-level language here) for acceptable performance gets smaller and smaller every day as hardware improves over time.
It's amazing to me that no matter how much memory we add, how much faster we make our CPUs, and how much faster we make our disks spin and seek, computing doesn't seem to get faster.
These baseline footprints seem totally reasonable to me, particularly considering the incredible productivity I get in exchange.
key_insight: Developer productivity should be valued over raw application performance because hardware improves continuously while human cognitive capacity does not.
